answer:
Definition

Architecting Refresh Tokens for 1M+ users requires a token rotation system that handles high-throughput refresh requests, detects token theft via family tracking, manages storage efficiently (millions of token records), and provides instant revocation capability across distributed services.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled ContactsHub with 1M users, Manoj would design: refresh tokens stored as SHA-256 hashes in MongoDB (indexed by userId and familyId), token families for reuse detection, a Redis-based rate limiter on the refresh endpoint (max 1 refresh per 10 seconds per user to prevent refresh storms), and automatic cleanup of expired tokens via MongoDB TTL index.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// MongoDB schema for refresh tokens with TTL index
const refreshTokenSchema = new mongoose.Schema({
  tokenHash: { type: String, required: true, unique: true, index: true },
  userId: { type: String, required: true, index: true },
  familyId: { type: String, required: true, index: true },
  used: { type: Boolean, default: false },
  createdAt: { type: Date, default: Date.now },
  expiresAt: { type: Date, required: true, index: { expireAfterSeconds: 0 } }, // TTL index
});

// Rate-limited refresh endpoint
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!refreshToken) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();

  // Rate limit: 1 refresh per 10 seconds per cookie fingerprint
  const fingerprint = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex').slice(0, 16);
  const rateLimitKey = `refresh-limit:${fingerprint}`;
  const attempts = await redis.incr(rateLimitKey);
  if (attempts === 1) await redis.expire(rateLimitKey, 10);
  if (attempts > 1) throw fastify.httpErrors.tooManyRequests('Refresh rate limited');

  const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
  const stored = await RefreshToken.findOne({ tokenHash });

  if (!stored || stored.expiresAt < new Date()) {
    if (stored) {
      // Expired but exists — clean up family
      await RefreshToken.deleteMany({ familyId: stored.familyId });
    }
    throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();
  }

  // Reuse detection
  if (stored.used) {
    // This token was already rotated — someone is replaying it!
    await RefreshToken.deleteMany({ familyId: stored.familyId });
    fastify.log.warn({ userId: stored.userId, familyId: stored.familyId }, 'Token reuse detected!');
    // Optionally: force re-login, send security alert email
    throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized('Security: please log in again');
  }

  // Mark as used (atomically)
  await RefreshToken.updateOne({ _id: stored._id }, { used: true });

  // Issue new token pair
  const user = await User.findById(stored.userId);
  const accessToken = await issueAccessToken(user);
  const newRefreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('base64url');
  const newTokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(newRefreshToken).digest('hex');

  await RefreshToken.create({
    tokenHash: newTokenHash,
    userId: user.id,
    familyId: stored.familyId, // Same family for reuse tracking
    expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 3600 * 1000),
  });

  reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
    httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict',
    path: '/api/auth', maxAge: 7 * 24 * 3600,
  });

  return { accessToken };
});

// Cleanup job: remove orphaned token families
async function cleanupOrphanedFamilies() {
  const thirtyDaysAgo = new Date(Date.now() - 30 * 24 * 3600 * 1000);
  const result = await RefreshToken.deleteMany({
    createdAt: { $lt: thirtyDaysAgo },
  });
  console.log(`Cleaned up ${result.deletedCount} expired token records`);
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: At 500K users, the MongoDB refreshTokens collection had grown to 50M documents (100× expected). Token refresh requests were taking 2s due to collection scans.

Investigation: Manoj checked the TTL index — it was configured on createdAt with expireAfterSeconds: 604800 (7 days). However, many users had been refreshing tokens continuously for months, creating new records without the old ones being deleted. The TTL was based on creation date, but rotated tokens had used: true and should have been cleaned up immediately.

Fix: (1) Changed TTL index to use expiresAt field (explicit expiry per token). (2) Added a post-rotation cleanup: after issuing a new token, delete all used: true tokens in the same family older than 1 hour. (3) Added a compound index on { userId: 1, familyId: 1 } for efficient lookups. (4) Collection dropped from 50M to 1.2M documents. Refresh time: 2s → 15ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Token collection grows unboundedTTL index on expiresAt; cleanup used tokens in rotation
Refresh storm (client retries on slow response)Rate limit: 1 refresh per 10 seconds per token
Token theft goes undetectedFamily-based reuse detection; invalidate chain on reuse
Missing index causes slow lookups at scaleCompound index on tokenHash, userId, familyId

Validation Strategy

  • Collection size: monitor document count, alert if exceeds 2× active user count
  • TTL verification: insert a test token with 1-minute TTL, verify auto-deletion
  • Reuse detection: use a rotated token, verify family invalidation + security log
  • Rate limit test: send 3 refresh requests in 10 seconds, verify 429 on 2nd and 3rd

Definition

Refresh Token performance bottlenecks surface when the token rotation system can't keep up with request volume: database contention during concurrent refreshes, race conditions in token rotation, slow hash computation, and connection pool exhaustion on the auth service during login spikes.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, every weekday at 9:00 AM, 10K employees simultaneously opened the Angular dashboard. Their access tokens (issued the previous day) had expired, triggering 10K concurrent refresh requests within 60 seconds. The auth service's PostgreSQL connection pool (20 connections) was overwhelmed, and many refresh requests failed with connection timeout errors.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Client-side: Pre-refresh before token expires (prevent 9 AM spike)
// Angular HTTP interceptor
@Injectable()
export class TokenRefreshInterceptor implements HttpInterceptor {
  private refreshing = false;

  intercept(req: HttpRequest, next: HttpHandler) {
    const token = this.authService.getAccessToken();
    if (token && this.isAboutToExpire(token, 120)) { // Refresh 2 min before expiry
      if (!this.refreshing) {
        this.refreshing = true;
        // Add jitter: random delay 0-30 seconds
        const jitter = Math.random() * 30000;
        setTimeout(() => {
          this.authService.refresh().finally(() => this.refreshing = false);
        }, jitter);
      }
    }
    return next.handle(req);
  }

  private isAboutToExpire(token: string, bufferSeconds: number): boolean {
    const payload = JSON.parse(atob(token.split('.')[1]));
    return payload.exp - Date.now() / 1000 < bufferSeconds;
  }
}

// Server-side: Batch-friendly refresh with PgBouncer
app.post('/api/auth/refresh', async (req, reply) => {
  const refreshToken = req.cookies.refreshToken;
  if (!refreshToken) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();

  // Deduplicate concurrent refreshes for same token
  const tokenFingerprint = crypto.createHash('sha256')
    .update(refreshToken).digest('hex').slice(0, 16);

  // Use Redis lock to prevent race condition
  const lockKey = `refresh-lock:${tokenFingerprint}`;
  const locked = await redis.set(lockKey, '1', { NX: true, EX: 10 });
  if (!locked) {
    // Another request is already refreshing this token — wait for result
    await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 1000));
    // The first request should have set the new token by now
    throw fastify.httpErrors.tooManyRequests('Refresh in progress, retry');
  }

  try {
    const tokenHash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(refreshToken).digest('hex');
    // Single optimized query: find + validate + mark used atomically
    const stored = await prisma.refreshToken.updateMany({
      where: { tokenHash, used: false, expiresAt: { gt: new Date() } },
      data: { used: true },
    });

    if (stored.count === 0) throw fastify.httpErrors.unauthorized();

    // Issue new tokens (non-blocking DB write)
    const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ where: { id: stored.userId } });
    const accessToken = await issueAccessToken(user);
    const newRefreshToken = crypto.randomBytes(64).toString('base64url');

    // Fire-and-forget: write new token to DB
    prisma.refreshToken.create({
      data: {
        tokenHash: crypto.createHash('sha256').update(newRefreshToken).digest('hex'),
        userId: user.id,
        familyId: stored.familyId,
        expiresAt: new Date(Date.now() + 7 * 24 * 3600 * 1000),
      },
    }).catch(err => fastify.log.error(err, 'Failed to persist new refresh token'));

    reply.setCookie('refreshToken', newRefreshToken, {
      httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict',
      path: '/api/auth', maxAge: 7 * 24 * 3600,
    });

    return { accessToken };
  } finally {
    await redis.del(lockKey);
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: Every morning at 9 AM, the HR system experienced a 3-minute period where 40% of login attempts failed. Users had to refresh their browser multiple times before getting in.

Investigation: Manoj correlated the failures with the token refresh spike. All 10K employees' access tokens expired overnight (issued during the previous workday, 15-min expiry with refresh). At 9 AM, the Angular app detected expired tokens and sent refresh requests simultaneously. The PostgreSQL connection pool (20 connections) queued 10K refresh requests, with each taking 50ms (hash + lookup + write). Throughput: 400 refreshes/second → 25 seconds to clear the queue. But PgBouncer timeout was 10s, so 6K requests timed out.

Fix: (1) Client-side pre-refresh: Angular refreshes tokens 2 minutes before expiry with random jitter (0-30s), spreading the spike over 30 seconds. (2) PgBouncer in transaction mode: multiplexed 100 app connections to 30 real PostgreSQL connections. (3) Atomic update (find + mark used in one query) instead of two round-trips. (4) Fire-and-forget new token write. Result: 9 AM refresh spike handled in < 5 seconds.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Thundering herd on token expiryClient-side jitter + pre-refresh before expiry window
Race condition: two requests refresh same tokenRedis distributed lock per token fingerprint
DB connection pool exhausted during spikePgBouncer transaction pooling; atomic queries
Token write failure leaves user without refresh tokenReturn access token immediately; retry token persistence in background

Validation Strategy

  • Spike simulation: send 10K concurrent refresh requests in 60s, verify > 99% success rate
  • Jitter verification: monitor refresh request distribution, verify spread over 30s window (not spike)
  • Race condition test: send 5 concurrent refreshes for same token, verify only 1 succeeds
  • Connection pool: monitor PgBouncer queue depth during spike, verify < 50 waiting

Definition

Socket.io Rooms performance bottlenecks at scale involve room join/leave overhead, broadcast amplification (N messages for N room members), memory consumption for large room memberships, and adapter synchronization across multiple server instances. When rooms contain thousands of members, each broadcast creates thousands of individual WebSocket writes.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj used Socket.io rooms for department-level notifications: when a leave is approved, all members of that department's room receive a real-time update. With 500 departments averaging 2000 employees each, the largest department (Operations) had 8000 members. Broadcasting to this room created 8000 individual WebSocket frame writes, causing a 2-second delay on the broadcasting server instance.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

import { Server } from 'socket.io';
import { createAdapter } from '@socket.io/redis-adapter';
import { createClient } from 'redis';
import msgpack from 'socket.io-msgpack-parser';

const io = new Server(server, {
  parser: msgpack,                    // Binary protocol: 30% smaller frames
  perMessageDeflate: true,            // Compress WebSocket frames
  maxHttpBufferSize: 1e6,             // 1MB max payload
  connectTimeout: 10000,
  adapter: createAdapter(pubClient, subClient),
});

// Optimized room broadcast with batching
async function broadcastToRoom(roomName: string, event: string, data: any) {
  const sockets = await io.in(roomName).fetchSockets();
  const batchSize = 100;

  for (let i = 0; i < sockets.length; i += batchSize) {
    const batch = sockets.slice(i, i + batchSize);
    batch.forEach(socket => socket.emit(event, data));

    // Yield to event loop between batches
    if (i + batchSize < sockets.length) {
      await new Promise(resolve => setImmediate(resolve));
    }
  }
}

// Alternative: Use volatile for non-critical notifications
function broadcastNotification(departmentId: string, notification: any) {
  // volatile = drop if client is not ready (no buffering)
  io.to(`dept:${departmentId}`).volatile.emit('notification', notification);
}

// Hierarchical rooms for scalable broadcasting
io.on('connection', (socket) => {
  const { departmentId, role, userId } = socket.handshake.auth;

  // User-level room (direct messages)
  socket.join(`user:${userId}`);

  // Department-level room (team notifications)
  socket.join(`dept:${departmentId}`);

  // Role-level room (role-specific announcements)
  socket.join(`role:${role}`);

  // Company-wide room (all employees)
  socket.join('company:all');
});

// Leave approval notification — targeted room
async function notifyLeaveApproval(leave: any) {
  // Notify the employee directly (1 message)
  io.to(`user:${leave.employeeId}`).emit('leave-status', {
    leaveId: leave.id,
    status: 'APPROVED',
    message: 'Your leave has been approved',
  });

  // Notify department managers (small room, ~5 members)
  io.to(`dept:${leave.departmentId}:managers`).emit('team-update', {
    type: 'leave-approved',
    employeeName: leave.employeeName,
    dates: `${leave.startDate} - ${leave.endDate}`,
  });

  // Don't broadcast to entire department room for individual leaves
  // Only broadcast summary events (e.g., weekly leave calendar update)
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: When an HR admin sent a company-wide announcement via Socket.io, the entire API became unresponsive for 5 seconds. Even non-WebSocket HTTP requests were blocked.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the event loop during the broadcast. The io.to('company:all').emit() call wrote to all 50K connected sockets synchronously. Each write involved serializing the JSON message (1KB), compressing it, and writing to the socket — 50K × 1KB = 50MB of writes blocking the event loop.

Fix: (1) Batched broadcasts: emit to 100 sockets at a time with setImmediate() yield between batches. (2) Switched to msgpack parser (30% smaller frames). (3) Used volatile flag for announcements (drop if client busy). (4) For company-wide messages, used a fan-out pattern: publish to Redis, each server instance broadcasts only to its local sockets. (5) For large announcements, sent an event telling clients to fetch the content via HTTP (instead of embedding the full message in the WebSocket frame). Result: broadcast time from 5s to 200ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Large room broadcasts block event loopBatch writes with setImmediate() yield every 100 sockets
Memory grows with room membership trackingHierarchical rooms; avoid putting all users in one room
Redis adapter serialization overheadmsgpack parser (binary) instead of JSON; compress frames
Client reconnection storm fills rooms uncontrollablyRate limit room joins; reconnection backoff with jitter

Validation Strategy

  • Broadcast test: emit to 10K-member room, verify event loop delay < 100ms
  • Memory test: create 500 rooms with 2K members each, verify memory < 500MB
  • Cross-instance test: emit from instance 1, verify delivery on instance 4 via Redis adapter
  • Volatile test: disconnect client, send volatile event, reconnect — verify no buffered delivery

Definition

An interviewer-grade deep dive into Socket.io Rooms examines the room abstraction for group messaging, adapter patterns for multi-instance deployment, namespace vs. room trade-offs, persistence strategies for offline users, failure modes (adapter desync, room leaks, message ordering), and rollback plans for real-time communication changes.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj used Socket.io rooms for real-time deal updates: each sales team has a room where deal stage changes are broadcast. When a deal moves from "qualified" to "proposal," all team members see the pipeline update instantly. Rooms: team:{teamId} (5-20 members), deal:{dealId} (watchers), notifications:{userId} (personal). The challenge: ensuring message ordering when deal updates arrive from multiple sales reps simultaneously.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Namespace-based organization with room management
const salesNamespace = io.of('/sales');

salesNamespace.use(async (socket, next) => {
  // Auth middleware — verify JWT for WebSocket connections
  const token = socket.handshake.auth.token;
  try {
    const payload = await verifyJWT(token);
    socket.data.user = payload;
    next();
  } catch {
    next(new Error('Authentication failed'));
  }
});

salesNamespace.on('connection', (socket) => {
  const { teamId, userId } = socket.data.user;

  // Auto-join team room
  socket.join(`team:${teamId}`);

  // Watch specific deals
  socket.on('watch-deal', (dealId: string) => {
    socket.join(`deal:${dealId}`);
  });

  socket.on('unwatch-deal', (dealId: string) => {
    socket.leave(`deal:${dealId}`);
  });

  // Handle disconnection — Socket.io auto-removes from rooms
  socket.on('disconnect', (reason) => {
    console.log(`User ${userId} disconnected: ${reason}`);
  });
});

// Emit deal update with ordering guarantee
let dealSequence = new Map(); // Per-deal sequence counter

async function emitDealUpdate(dealId: string, update: any) {
  const seq = (dealSequence.get(dealId) || 0) + 1;
  dealSequence.set(dealId, seq);

  const event = {
    ...update,
    _seq: seq,          // Client can detect out-of-order
    _timestamp: Date.now(),
  };

  // Emit to deal watchers
  salesNamespace.to(`deal:${dealId}`).emit('deal-update', event);

  // Also emit to the deal owner's team room
  const deal = await db.collection('deals').findOne({ _id: dealId });
  if (deal) {
    salesNamespace.to(`team:${deal.teamId}`).emit('pipeline-change', {
      dealId,
      stage: update.newStage,
      value: deal.value,
      _seq: seq,
    });
  }
}

// Offline message persistence
async function emitWithPersistence(userId: string, event: string, data: any) {
  const sockets = await salesNamespace.in(`user:${userId}`).fetchSockets();
  if (sockets.length > 0) {
    // User is online — deliver immediately
    salesNamespace.to(`user:${userId}`).emit(event, data);
  } else {
    // User is offline — store for later delivery
    await redis.lPush(`offline:${userId}`, JSON.stringify({ event, data, timestamp: Date.now() }));
    await redis.expire(`offline:${userId}`, 7 * 24 * 3600); // 7-day retention
  }
}

// On reconnection — deliver queued messages
salesNamespace.on('connection', async (socket) => {
  const { userId } = socket.data.user;
  const queued = await redis.lRange(`offline:${userId}`, 0, -1);
  if (queued.length > 0) {
    for (const msg of queued) {
      const { event, data } = JSON.parse(msg);
      socket.emit(event, data);
    }
    await redis.del(`offline:${userId}`);
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: CRM Dashboard users reported seeing deal updates out of order — a deal would show as "closed_won" then flash back to "proposal" before settling on "closed_won." This caused confusion about the actual pipeline state.

Investigation: Manoj found that two sales reps updated the same deal simultaneously from different browser tabs. Both updates were processed by different Socket.io server instances. The Redis adapter published both events to all instances, but network latency caused instance-2's update to arrive before instance-1's on some clients, even though instance-1's update happened first chronologically.

Fix: (1) Added a per-deal sequence number. Each update increments the deal's sequence counter (atomic increment in Redis). (2) Client-side checks: if received event's _seq is less than the last seen sequence, ignore it. (3) Added server-side optimistic concurrency: updates include the expected current stage, rejecting if the deal has already moved past that stage. (4) For critical state changes, client fetches latest state via HTTP after receiving the WebSocket event (event as hint, HTTP as truth).

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Out-of-order message delivery across instancesPer-entity sequence numbers; client-side ordering filter
Offline users miss events permanentlyPersist missed events in Redis list; deliver on reconnection
Room membership leaks on ungraceful disconnectSocket.io auto-cleanup; periodic room audit for stale members
Real-time feature breaks → users see stale dataFeature flag: fall back to HTTP polling if WebSocket fails

Validation Strategy

  • Ordering test: send 100 rapid updates to same deal from 2 instances, verify client sees correct final state
  • Offline test: disconnect user, send 5 events, reconnect → verify all 5 delivered in order
  • Room cleanup: disconnect 100 clients ungracefully, verify rooms have 0 stale members after 30s
  • Fallback test: kill Socket.io server, verify Angular client switches to HTTP polling within 10s

Definition

Architecting API Versioning for 1M+ users means designing a versioning strategy that allows simultaneous operation of multiple API versions, safe deprecation of old versions, zero-downtime migration of clients between versions, and efficient code organization that avoids version-specific code sprawl.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled HR Leave Management System, Manoj needs to evolve the leave API: v1 returns flat leave objects, v2 adds nested approval chains and department hierarchies, v3 will support multi-timezone leave calculations. With 1M users on different client versions (Angular web on v2, mobile on v1, third-party integrations on v1), all versions must coexist for 6 months during migration.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// URL-based versioning with shared service layer
// Shared domain logic (version-agnostic)
class LeaveService {
  async getLeaves(userId: string, options?: { includeApprovals?: boolean }) {
    const leaves = await prisma.leave.findMany({
      where: { employeeId: userId },
      include: options?.includeApprovals ? { approvals: true } : undefined,
    });
    return leaves;
  }
}

// V1 handler — flat response
app.register(async (v1) => {
  v1.get('/leaves', async (req) => {
    const leaves = await leaveService.getLeaves(req.user.id);
    // V1 response shape: flat objects
    return leaves.map(l => ({
      id: l.id,
      startDate: l.startDate,
      endDate: l.endDate,
      status: l.status,
      type: l.type,
      approvedBy: l.approvedBy, // Flat field
    }));
  });

  // Sunset header on all v1 responses
  v1.addHook('onSend', (req, reply, payload, done) => {
    reply.header('Sunset', 'Sat, 01 Jun 2025 00:00:00 GMT');
    reply.header('Deprecation', 'true');
    reply.header('Link', '; rel="successor-version"');
    done();
  });
}, { prefix: '/api/v1' });

// V2 handler — nested response with approval chain
app.register(async (v2) => {
  v2.get('/leaves', async (req) => {
    const leaves = await leaveService.getLeaves(req.user.id, { includeApprovals: true });
    // V2 response shape: nested objects
    return leaves.map(l => ({
      id: l.id,
      dates: { start: l.startDate, end: l.endDate, days: l.days },
      status: l.status,
      type: l.type,
      approvalChain: l.approvals?.map(a => ({
        level: a.level,
        approver: { id: a.approverId, name: a.approverName },
        status: a.status,
        timestamp: a.updatedAt,
      })),
    }));
  });
}, { prefix: '/api/v2' });

// Version usage monitoring
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req) => {
  const version = req.url.match(/\/api\/(v\d+)/)?.[1] || 'unknown';
  metrics.increment('api_version_requests', { version });

  // Alert if deprecated version still has significant traffic
  if (version === 'v1') {
    const count = await redis.incr('v1-daily-count');
    if (count === 1) await redis.expire('v1-daily-count', 86400);
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After deploying v2 of the leave API, third-party HR analytics integrations broke. They were using v1 endpoints that now returned v2 response shapes (nested approval chains instead of flat approvedBy field).

Investigation: Manoj found that during the v2 deployment, a developer had modified the shared LeaveService.getLeaves() to always include approval chains. The v1 handler didn't explicitly select only v1 fields — it passed through whatever the service returned. When the service added the approvals array, v1 responses grew from 500 bytes to 3KB, and the approvedBy flat field was removed in favor of the nested structure.

Fix: (1) Made v1 handler explicitly map to v1 response shape (defensive transformation). (2) Added response schema validation per version — if the response doesn't match the version's JSON Schema, return 500 instead of sending broken data. (3) Created contract tests for each version that run in CI. (4) Added a version compatibility matrix in the codebase documenting which service methods each version handler uses.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Shared service change breaks old version handlersExplicit response mapping per version; contract tests
Version code duplication grows maintenance burdenShared service layer; version handlers only transform responses
Deprecated version never removed (zombie code)Usage monitoring; auto-alert when deprecated version drops below 1%
Clients don't notice deprecation warningsSunset and Deprecation headers; email notification to integration partners

Validation Strategy

  • Contract test: verify v1 and v2 responses match their respective JSON Schemas
  • Backward compatibility: change service layer, verify v1 response shape unchanged
  • Deprecation headers: verify v1 responses include Sunset + Deprecation headers
  • Usage monitoring: dashboard showing daily request count per version

Definition

API Versioning performance bottlenecks arise from version-specific routing overhead, response transformation layers, maintaining multiple serialization schemas, and the operational cost of running parallel code paths. At scale, each version adds CPU overhead for routing decisions and memory for cached schemas.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj maintained 3 API versions (v1, v2, v3) for backward compatibility with different client apps. The version router checked every request against a version map, and each version had its own response serialization schema. With 3 versions × 50 endpoints × 10K req/s = 30K routing decisions per second, the regex-based version parser added 2ms per request.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Optimized version routing with Fastify prefix tree
// Fastify uses a radix tree for routing — O(log n) instead of regex O(n)

// Pre-compiled response schemas per version
import fastJson from 'fast-json-stringify';

const v1LeaveSerializer = fastJson({
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    id: { type: 'string' },
    startDate: { type: 'string' },
    endDate: { type: 'string' },
    status: { type: 'string' },
    type: { type: 'string' },
    approvedBy: { type: 'string' },
  },
});

const v2LeaveSerializer = fastJson({
  type: 'object',
  properties: {
    id: { type: 'string' },
    dates: {
      type: 'object',
      properties: {
        start: { type: 'string' },
        end: { type: 'string' },
        days: { type: 'number' },
      },
    },
    status: { type: 'string' },
    type: { type: 'string' },
    approvalChain: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        properties: {
          level: { type: 'number' },
          status: { type: 'string' },
        },
      },
    },
  },
});

// Register with pre-compiled serializers
app.register(async (v1) => {
  v1.get('/leaves', {
    schema: {
      response: { 200: { type: 'array', items: { $ref: 'v1Leave' } } },
    },
  }, async (req) => {
    const leaves = await leaveService.getLeaves(req.user.id);
    return leaves.map(mapToV1); // Explicit transformation
  });
}, { prefix: '/api/v1' });

// Response transformation functions (no dynamic schema lookup)
function mapToV1(leave: DBLeave) {
  return {
    id: leave.id,
    startDate: leave.startDate.toISOString(),
    endDate: leave.endDate.toISOString(),
    status: leave.status,
    type: leave.type,
    approvedBy: leave.approvedBy || null,
  };
}

function mapToV2(leave: DBLeave) {
  return {
    id: leave.id,
    dates: {
      start: leave.startDate.toISOString(),
      end: leave.endDate.toISOString(),
      days: leave.days,
    },
    status: leave.status,
    type: leave.type,
    approvalChain: leave.approvals?.map(a => ({
      level: a.level,
      status: a.status,
    })),
  };
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: CRM API p95 latency increased by 3ms after adding v3 endpoints. The v3 endpoints themselves were fast, but all endpoints (v1 and v2) got slower.

Investigation: Manoj profiled the routing layer. A custom version middleware was checking each request URL against a regex for version extraction before Fastify's internal router. This regex ran on every request, and adding v3 patterns made the regex more complex. Additionally, the response serialization was using JSON.stringify with runtime schema selection instead of pre-compiled serializers.

Fix: (1) Removed the custom version regex middleware — Fastify's built-in prefix routing already handles /api/v1, /api/v2, /api/v3 via radix tree (0.01ms). (2) Pre-compiled response serializers with fast-json-stringify at startup — 3× faster than JSON.stringify for known schemas. (3) Lazy-loaded v1 route handlers (since only 2% of traffic). Result: 3ms overhead eliminated.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Custom version routing adds per-request overheadUse framework's built-in prefix routing (radix tree)
Runtime schema selection for serializationPre-compile response serializers per version at startup
Old version code loaded in memory even if unusedLazy-load deprecated version handlers
Response transformation CPU costCache transformed responses for read-heavy endpoints (Redis, 30s TTL)

Validation Strategy

  • Routing benchmark: measure per-request routing overhead, verify < 0.1ms
  • Serialization benchmark: compare JSON.stringify vs. fast-json-stringify for each version schema
  • Version isolation: add v4, verify zero performance impact on v1/v2/v3
  • Memory audit: check heap for version-specific code, verify unused versions don't consume memory
